aliases: Vietnamese dry vermicelli with grilled beef

english: Vietnamese dry vermicelli with grilled beef

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Vietnamese charbroiled ribeye steak vermicelli specifically; mapped to broader noodle dish class FOODON_00002473.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) used as closest defensible parent; no exact code exists for Vietnamese dry vermicelli with charbroiled ribeye.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of charbroiled ribeye steak vermicelli weighing approximately 550 g, comprising rice vermicelli noodles, sliced charbroiled ribeye steak, fresh vegetables and herbs, fried shallots, and nuoc cham dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy composite estimate for Vietnamese-style grilled beef vermicelli dish (beef ribeye FDC #174032; cooked rice vermicelli FDC #168917)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for charbroiled ribeye steak vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data: beef ribeye (FDC #174032), cooked rice vermicelli (FDC #168917), and fresh mixed vegetables and herbs, weighted to reflect a typical Vietnamese dry vermicelli bowl composition. Input per_100g values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite estimate and found to be internally consistent; they have been retained with medium confidence. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated from rice vermicelli (GI 53, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008), which is the dominant carbohydrate source. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 53 falls in the low category. The presence of protein (8.2 g/100g from ribeye steak) and dietary fibre (0.9 g/100g) from vegetables further moderates postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 59.95) / 100 ≈ 31.8 g, where available carbohydrate per serving = 10.9 g/100g × 550 g = 59.95 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 272.7 mg/100g; estimated 1,500 mg per 550 g serving from fish sauce, soy sauce marinade, and nuoc cham
hypertension.guidance: Request nuoc cham dipping sauce on the side and use sparingly; limit portion to half a bowl to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg, and choose a low-sodium soy sauce marinade where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli GI 53 (low category); carbohydrate 10.9 g/100g with glycemic load approximately 31.8 g per full 550 g serving, which is high in absolute terms
type2_diabetes.guidance: Limit portion to approximately 300 g to keep glycemic load below 18 g per meal; pair with additional non-starchy vegetables and request less sugar in the marinade to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ribeye steak contributes saturated fat 2.2 g/100g (approximately 12.1 g per serving) and cholesterol 21.8 mg/100g (approximately 120 mg per serving); sodium load is also elevated
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Consume no more than half a bowl (275 g) to limit saturated fat to approximately 6 g and sodium to approximately 750 mg per meal; request sauce on the side and consider substituting ribeye with a leaner cut such as sirloin to reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ribeye steak is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways including TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid ribeye steak due to its high arachidonic acid content; consider substituting with grilled chicken breast or tofu and requesting fried shallots be omitted to reduce advanced glycation end-product (AGE) intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Charbroiled Ribeye Steak Vermicelli (招牌越南牛排干米粉; Vietnamese: Bún Bò Nướng) is a Vietnamese-inspired dry noodle dish served in Singapore. The dish features thinly sliced charbroiled ribeye steak — marinated in a soy-based sauce with garlic and lemongrass, then grilled over high heat to develop a smoky char — served over a bed of cooked rice vermicelli noodles. It is typically assembled with fresh bean sprouts, shredded lettuce or cucumber, fresh mint and coriander, crispy fried shallots, and crushed peanuts, with nuoc cham (Vietnamese fish sauce dipping sauce) served on the side. The texture profile combines the tender, slightly chewy char-grilled beef with soft, springy vermicelli and crisp fresh vegetables. The flavour is savoury, umami-rich, and mildly smoky, with herbal brightness from the fresh herbs and a tangy-sweet-salty finish from the nuoc cham. Nutritionally, a 550 g serving provides approximately 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 35 g fat, and 60 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at approximately 1,500 mg per serving due to fish sauce and soy sauce marinade, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is 53 (low category) based on rice vermicelli; however, the glycemic load per full serving is approximately 31.8 g, which is high in absolute terms, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 300 g. The ribeye cut contributes saturated fat (approximately 12 g per serving) and arachidonic acid, which is relevant for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for beef ribeye (FDC #174032), cooked rice vermicelli (FDC #168917), and fresh vegetables, cross-checked against the input grabfood values (confidence 0.7). The composite estimate is internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (beef ribeye FDC #174032, cooked rice vermicelli FDC #168917, mixed fresh vegetables) weighted to a typical Vietnamese dry vermicelli bowl composition. Input grabfood values (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and retained as they are consistent with the component estimate. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese-style dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.5 g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5 g/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ribeye steak fatty acid profile and are approximate. 4. potassium_mg (220 mg/100g) is estimated from vegetable and peanut components per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce (non-halal certified) is a standard ingredient; halal-certified versions may exist. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); GI 53 is classified as low (≤55) per standard GI classification. No direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 7. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. 8. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration: GI 53 falls in the low category (≤55) per the standard University of Sydney GI classification scale.
